metadata.working_title: RNAseqCovarImpute: A Multiple Imputation Procedure for Differential Expression Analysis with Missing Covariate Data

abstract_sketch.problem: Missing covariate data in observational transcriptomic studies undermines differential expression analyses, yet is rarely handled rigorously.
abstract_sketch.gap: Standard multiple imputation (MI) procedures cannot accommodate the high dimensionality of RNA-seq outcomes; researchers typically default to complete case (CC) analysis or single imputation (SI), both of which can bias estimates.
abstract_sketch.approach: We present RNAseqCovarImpute, an R package that bins genes into small groups, constructs M imputed datasets separately within each bin using the outcome genes as predictors, fits limma-voom models per bin and per imputation, and pools results with Rubin's rules.
abstract_sketch.key_results: Across three simulation studies (N=1,044 with MCAR/MAR missingness at 5-55% levels and null-gene rates of 82.5% and 50%), RNAseqCovarImpute identified 95.6-98.5% of true DEGs under MCAR versus 56.6-90.7% for SI, with comparable FDR (2.9-13.8% vs 1.9-14.8%). Applied to 1,045 ECHO-PATHWAYS placental samples, the MI analysis revealed 382 maternal-age DEGs, with 30 unique to MI.
abstract_sketch.impact: RNAseqCovarImpute enables principled handling of missing covariates in human observational transcriptomic studies and integrates directly with limma-voom.

sections.title: Introduction
content_bullets: Missing covariate data is ubiquitous in observational studies and degrades linear models used in differential expression analysis.
Complete case analysis drops participants, reducing power and introducing bias.
Single imputation ignores predictive uncertainty and produces over-confident standard errors.
Multiple imputation addresses uncertainty via M imputed datasets pooled by Rubin's rules, but requires including the outcome in the predictor matrix.
Including tens of thousands of genes as outcomes in a single MI model is infeasible when the number of predictors exceeds participants.
Prior DNA-methylation work addressed this by binning CpG sites; no analogous method exists for RNA-seq.
We contribute RNAseqCovarImpute, an R package built around gene binning and limma-voom, and evaluate it on three simulation studies plus a real placental RNA-seq application.

subsections.title: Gene binning and imputation
content_bullets: Default bin size is approximately 1 gene per 10 participants; user-configurable.
For 500 participants and 10,000 genes, the method constructs 200 bins of 50 genes.
When gene counts are not divisible by bin size, flexible bins of two sizes are created (e.g., 199 bins of 50 and 1 bin of 51 for 10,001 genes).
Gene identifiers are randomized prior to binning.

Within each bin, M imputed datasets are constructed via the mice package with default predictive mean matching, logistic regression, polytomous regression, and proportional-odds modeling for continuous, binary, categorical, and ordered variables.

subsections.title: Differential expression analysis
content_bullets: The voom mean-variance trend is estimated across all genes in all M imputations using the full pooled residuals and log-counts.
A modified voom injects these global precision weights into bin-specific lmFit calls.
lmFit is applied separately per bin per imputation; model tables are then un-binned and stacked into M gene-wise result sets.
squeezeVar is applied separately per M set to execute empirical Bayes variance shrinkage.
Rubin's rules pool coefficients, standard errors, and Barnard-Rubin adjusted degrees of freedom; Wald statistics and two-sided P-values are returned.
FDR adjustment is applied to the pooled P-values.

subsections.title: Simulation design
content_bullets: Simulation 1 uses real ECHO-PATHWAYS placental RNA-seq and covariate data with N=1,044, 14,027 genes.
Simulation 2 replaces counts with seqgendiff synthetic signal at 82.5% and 50% null-gene rates (2,000 perturbed genes).
Simulation 3 uses fully synthetic covariates (V1-V5 with Spearman rho = 0.1, 0.2, 0.3, 0.4) at sample sizes 500, 200, 100, 50.
Missingness induced via mice::ampute at 5-55% under MCAR and MAR; ten datasets per scenario.
CC drops incomplete rows; SI uses missForest; MI uses RNAseqCovarImpute.
Metrics: percent of true DEGs recovered, FDR, and bias relative to the full-data model coefficients.

subsections.title: Placental transcriptome application
content_bullets: N=1,045 ECHO-PATHWAYS individuals with placental RNA-seq (14,029 genes).
Predictor: maternal age; 10 covariates including race, ethnicity, education, tobacco, alcohol, income, delivery mode, fetal sex, sequencing batch, study site.
61 of 1,045 individuals (6%) were missing at least one covariate.

subsections.title: Simulation 1: real covariates, real counts
content_bullets: Full-data limma-voom identified 2,453 true DEGs.
Under MCAR, RNAseqCovarImpute recovered 95.6-98.5% of true DEGs versus SI 56.6-90.7%.
FDR was comparable across methods: RNAseqCovarImpute 2.9-13.8%, SI 1.9-14.8%, CC 5.3-12.8%.
Under MAR, RNAseqCovarImpute recovered 90.3-97.4% versus SI 55.7-77.6%; CC FDR climbed to 11.4-18.5% compared with MI 2.0-8.0% and SI 1.4-12.2%.
Bias was more tightly centered at zero for RNAseqCovarImpute in both all-gene and true-DEG subsets.

subsections.title: Simulation 2: real covariates, synthetic counts
content_bullets: At 82.5% null-gene rate under MCAR, RNAseqCovarImpute recovered 96.8-99.1% of true DEGs versus SI 78.9-95.9% and CC 58.2-92.9%.
MI and SI had comparable FDR (1.4-6.1% and 1.4-7.6%) while CC FDR was 3.0-8.0%.
Under MAR with the 82.5% null rate, percent true DEGs were 94.0-98.2% (MI), 77.6-89.6% (SI), 61.1-93.2% (CC); FDR ranges 0.9-3.9%, 1.2-7.7%, 6.0-10.9%.
At the 50% null-gene rate the same ordering (MI > SI > CC) was preserved across MCAR and MAR.

subsections.title: Simulation 3: synthetic covariates and counts
content_bullets: Across N in {500, 200, 100, 50} with covariate correlation 0.1, RNAseqCovarImpute was the top performer for true DEG recovery and FDR.
Bias for RNAseqCovarImpute was tighter around zero than SI or CC across all missingness mechanisms and levels.
Sensitivity analyses at rho = 0.2, 0.3, 0.4 preserved the MI advantage except at rho = 0.4 where SI recovered more true positives but with FDR exceeding 60% in one scenario.

subsections.title: Maternal age and placental transcriptome
content_bullets: Among 1,045 participants, 6% (61) had at least one missing covariate.
CC, SI, and MI identified 575, 214, and 382 DEGs, respectively.
CC captured 96% (368) and SI captured 56% (214) of MI-identified DEGs; 30 DEGs were MI-exclusive.
Top genes (S100A12, S100A8, CXCL8/IL-8, IL1R2, SAA1, CASC19, LILRA5) are involved in inflammation and innate immunity.

sections.title: Discussion
content_bullets: MI with outcome-informed predictor matrix outperformed CC and SI without the FDR tradeoff reported for methylation analyses.
Higher dimensionality of methylation (~850,000 CpGs) makes direct translation of this approach to EWAS more challenging than transcriptomics.
Observational RNA-seq studies increasingly resemble epidemiologic cohorts where missing covariates are prevalent but rarely reported.
Real-world application demonstrated that the missing-data method materially changes the set of DEGs reported.
Unadjusted analyses of maternal age and offspring outcomes (e.g., ADHD) can reverse sign once confounders are included, underscoring the need for covariate control.
Limitations: pathway and gene-set enrichment methods requiring sample-level matrices are not directly compatible; MI integrates cleanly with rank-based enrichment (camera, gage).
Out-of-box integration is limited to limma-voom, but the package exposes imputation and binning functions so users can plug into alternate pipelines.

sections.title: Conclusions
content_bullets: RNAseqCovarImpute bridges transcriptomic analysis and observational epidemiology by enabling principled handling of missing covariates.
Across simulation and real-data experiments, MI via RNAseqCovarImpute improved sensitivity while controlling FDR and reducing coefficient bias.
